subsystem: Vim editor screen/status-line rendering. The function win_redr_status() draws a window's status line: it builds a display string from the buffer name plus state flags ([Help], [Preview], [+], [RO]), truncates it to the available width, paints it with screen_puts()/screen_fill(), optionally overlays a keymap name, and draws the ruler and the vertical-separator character.
provenance: Identified by Vim-specific types and globals: char_u, win_T *wp, NameBuff, MAXPATHL, STRLEN, vim_snprintf, has_mbyte, mb_string2cells/mb_ptr2len/mb_ptr2cells, screen_puts/screen_fill/screen_putchar, redraw_custom_statusline, win_redr_ruler, and feature macros FEAT_STL_OPT/FEAT_QUICKFIX/FEAT_TERMINAL/FEAT_CMDL_INFO/FEAT_STL_OPT. The banner comment 'sample: primevul_c_test_0333_fixed' marks it as a PrimeVul-derived sample; the code is win_redr_status() from Vim's drawscreen.c/screen.c.
trust_boundary: This is an internal UI redraw routine, not a network/IPC parser, so it is far from a remote attack surface. Its inputs are local editor state: the window/buffer pointer wp (trusted structure), the buffer's display name produced by get_trans_bufname() into the global NameBuff, buffer state flags, the 'statusline' option strings, a keymap string from get_keymap_str(), and screen geometry (Columns, wp->w_width). The semi-attacker-influenced data is the buffer name / file path and the keymap string, which can be arbitrarily long and arbitrary bytes (a crafted filename or a file/modeline that sets a long statusline). Those flow into NameBuff (a fixed MAXPATHL buffer) and onto the visible status line, which is the CWE-200 information-surface aspect. The safety of every write depends on the invariant that len never exceeds MAXPATHL.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Function entry for win_redr_status(). It takes wp, the window whose status line is being drawn, and ignore_pum, a flag (marked UNUSED for builds where it is compiled out) that says whether the popup menu should be ignored when deciding to redraw. Locals: row is the screen row of the status line; p is a working pointer into the display string (usually NameBuff); len tracks the current byte length / display width of the string being built; fillchar and attr are the fill character and highlight attribute for the status line; this_ru_col is the column where the ruler starts. The key declaration is 'static int busy = FALSE;' — a function-scoped static used as a re-entrancy once-guard, described next.

The recursion guard. Because the 'statusline' option can be evaluated to build the status text and that evaluation can itself trigger ':redrawstatus', win_redr_status() can be entered recursively. The guard returns immediately if busy is already TRUE, then sets busy = TRUE for the duration of this call. This is exactly the once-guard the review checklist asks for against re-parse/recursion. Note this return at line 15 happens BEFORE busy is set, so it does not leave the flag stuck; every other path falls through to line 145 which clears it.

row is computed as the screen row of this window's status line. wp->w_redr_status is cleared to record that we are servicing the redraw request. The first branch handles a window with no status line (w_status_height == 0): there is nothing to draw, so it just marks the command line for redraw and (via the else-if chain) skips all drawing.

Second branch: if redrawing is currently suppressed (!redrawing()), or the popup menu is visible and we were not told to ignore it (!ignore_pum && pum_visible()) — meaning the popup might be painted over the status line — the function defers the work by re-setting wp->w_redr_status = TRUE so the redraw happens later, and draws nothing now.

Third branch (only when the statusline-option feature is compiled in): if a global 'statusline' (p_stl) or window-local one (wp->w_p_stl) is set to a non-empty string, drawing is delegated to redraw_custom_statusline(wp), which evaluates the user's statusline format. This is the code path that can recurse back into win_redr_status() and is the reason the busy guard exists.

The default branch draws the built-in status line. fillchar_status() picks the fill character and the highlight attribute (attr) for this window's status line. get_trans_bufname(wp->w_buffer) writes the buffer's (translated/truncated) display name into the global NameBuff buffer. p is pointed at NameBuff, and len is initialized to STRLEN(p) — the current byte length of the name. This establishes the central invariant for what follows: p points at a MAXPATHL-sized buffer and len is the used length, which callers assume stays below MAXPATHL.

what_the_code_does.code:
if ((bt_help(wp->w_buffer)
#ifdef FEAT_QUICKFIX
		    || wp->w_p_pvw
#endif
		    || bufIsChanged(wp->w_buffer)
		    || wp->w_buffer->b_p_ro)
		&& len < MAXPATHL - 1)
	    *(p + len++) = ' ';
what_the_code_does.explanation: If the buffer is a help buffer, a preview window (w_p_pvw), modified (bufIsChanged), or read-only (b_p_ro) — i.e. a state flag will be appended — a separating space is written after the name. Crucially this write is gated by 'len < MAXPATHL - 1', so the byte at p[len] is only written when there is room, and len is post-incremented. This is a correctly bounded single-byte append and is the pattern the rest of the function relies on to keep len within MAXPATHL.

If this is a help buffer, the '[Help]' tag is appended at p+len using vim_snprintf with a size argument of MAXPATHL - len — i.e. the number of bytes remaining in NameBuff. vim_snprintf NUL-terminates and never writes more than that many bytes, so the write is bounded provided len <= MAXPATHL. Then len is advanced by the length actually written (STRLEN(p + len)). The same append-and-advance idiom repeats for the next three flags.

Three more conditional tag appends, each using the identical bounded vim_snprintf(p+len, MAXPATHL-len, ...) then len += STRLEN(p+len) pattern: '[Preview]' for a preview window, '[+]' for a modified buffer (excluding terminal buffers), and '[RO]' for a read-only buffer. Because each write is capped at the remaining space and len is re-derived from the actual bytes written, len monotonically approaches but does not exceed MAXPATHL-1 as long as the incoming NameBuff was a valid NUL-terminated string shorter than MAXPATHL.

this_ru_col is where the ruler begins, computed from the global ruler column ru_col adjusted for this window's width relative to the whole screen (Columns). It is then clamped up to at least half the window width. If after clamping there is essentially no room (this_ru_col <= 1), the name is discarded and p is repointed at the string literal '<' with len = 1 — meaning only a truncation marker is shown. Note p now points at read-only static storage, so nothing writes through it in this branch.

The multibyte truncation path. clen is the total number of display cells of the string via mb_string2cells(p, -1). The for-loop walks the string one multibyte character at a time (advancing i by (*mb_ptr2len)(p+i)), subtracting each character's cell width from clen, until either the NUL terminator is reached (p[i] != NUL is the bound that keeps the walk inside the buffer) or the remaining cells fit within this_ru_col - 1. len is then set to the remaining cell count clen. If any characters were dropped (i > 0), p is advanced to p+i-1 and that byte is overwritten with '<' to show truncation, and len is incremented. The overwrite is inside the buffer because i is at most the string length. Note that after 'len = clen', len holds a display-cell count rather than a byte count.

The single-byte (non-multibyte) truncation path. If the byte length len exceeds the available columns (this_ru_col - 1), p is advanced by the overflow amount so the string's tail is shown, the leading visible byte is overwritten with '<', and len is set to exactly this_ru_col - 1. Because len > this_ru_col - 1 here, the shift is positive and smaller than len, so p stays within the buffer. In this branch a byte equals one column, so len remains simultaneously a byte length and a column count.

The assembled (and possibly truncated) string p is painted onto the status row starting at column wp->w_wincol with attribute attr. screen_puts writes until p's NUL terminator, so its bound is the string, not len. screen_fill then pads from column len + wp->w_wincol up to this_ru_col + wp->w_wincol with the fill character, filling the gap between the end of the name and the ruler. Here len is used as a COLUMN offset — which is why the multibyte path stored a cell count into len rather than a byte count.

If a keymap name exists, get_keymap_str() formats '<name>' back into NameBuff, explicitly bounded by MAXPATHL (its 4th argument is the buffer size). It is drawn only if there is room: (this_ru_col - len) must exceed STRLEN(NameBuff) + 1. If so, the keymap string is painted right-aligned before the ruler column. Note NameBuff is reused here, overwriting the buffer name — but p was already consumed by screen_puts at line 120, and in the truncation branches p may point elsewhere; the ordering makes this safe.

When the command-line-info feature is compiled in, win_redr_ruler() draws the ruler (cursor position info) for this window, honoring the same ignore_pum flag. This closes the default (built-in status line) branch of the outer if/else chain.

After the status line itself, if the window has a vertical separator (w_vsep_width != 0), a status line (w_status_height != 0), and redrawing is active, a single fill character is drawn at the join of the status line and the vertical separator: fillchar_status() if the status lines are connected, else fillchar_vsep(). Finally busy = FALSE releases the recursion guard set at line 16, ensuring subsequent calls proceed. Every non-early-return path reaches this reset.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The size argument MAXPATHL - len is computed in signed int (len is 'int'). vim_snprintf's size parameter is size_t (unsigned). The safety of all four tag-append snprintf calls (lines 59, 65, 75, 80) rests entirely on the invariant len <= MAXPATHL. If len ever became greater than MAXPATHL, MAXPATHL - len would be a negative int that converts to an enormous size_t, turning the bounded snprintf into an effectively unbounded write into the global NameBuff — a classic integer-underflow-to-buffer-overflow (CWE-191 -> CWE-787). In THIS function len is kept in range: get_trans_bufname() fills NameBuff (size MAXPATHL, NUL-terminated) so the initial STRLEN < MAXPATHL, the space append is guarded by 'len < MAXPATHL - 1', and each snprintf caps the write so len re-derived from STRLEN stays below MAXPATHL. So the risk is latent: it depends on get_trans_bufname() honoring the MAXPATHL bound and returning a properly terminated string. The implementation must uphold 'len <= MAXPATHL at every subtraction' for this to remain safe.

In the multibyte branch, len is assigned clen, a count of DISPLAY CELLS, whereas in the non-multibyte branch (line 117) and at initialization (line 47) len is a BYTE length. Downstream, len is used as a column offset in screen_fill (line 121, 'len + wp->w_wincol') and in the keymap room check (line 125, 'this_ru_col - len'). For column math a cell count is the intended unit, so this is self-consistent for rendering, but the dual meaning of len is fragile: any future code that treats len as a byte index into p would be wrong for the multibyte path and could read or write out of bounds. The loop itself is bounded by 'p[i] != NUL', so it does not over-read NameBuff, and 'p = p + i - 1; *p = '<'' writes within the buffer. Impact today is at most an incorrect fill width, not memory corruption — a low-severity correctness/robustness concern (CWE-125 only if a later consumer misuses len as a byte length).

The whole routine trusts get_trans_bufname() to leave NameBuff NUL-terminated and shorter than MAXPATHL. NameBuff is a shared global scratch buffer used all over Vim; win_redr_status() both reads it here and rewrites it at line 124 via get_keymap_str(). The information-exposure angle (CWE-200) is by design: a crafted or very long file/buffer name — attacker-influenced if Vim opens hostile filenames — is rendered onto the status line. There is no memory-safety bug from this in the shown code because every write into NameBuff is size-bounded, but the implementation must guarantee get_trans_bufname()/get_keymap_str() never overflow NameBuff and always terminate it; if either violated that, the STRLEN at line 47 and the STRLEN(p+len) advances would run past the buffer (CWE-125 over-read).

this_ru_col is derived by signed subtraction from ru_col, Columns and wp->w_width and can transiently go negative or large, but it is immediately clamped to at least (wp->w_width + 1) / 2 and then checked '<= 1' at line 87. Consequently the values fed to screen_puts/screen_fill column arguments are bounded by window geometry. The residual concern is that these columns are passed into screen_fill/screen_puts as start/end columns; if Columns/w_width are ever inconsistent the fill range 'len + wp->w_wincol' .. 'this_ru_col + wp->w_wincol' could be reversed, which those routines must tolerate. This is a robustness contract on the screen_* helpers, not an overflow in this function.

The static 'busy' re-entrancy guard is correct as written: the only early return (line 15) happens before busy is set, and every other path reaches 'busy = FALSE' at line 145. The failure mode to watch for in the implementation is a called helper (redraw_custom_statusline, screen_*, win_redr_ruler) that longjmp()s or throws out of the function without unwinding to line 145 — that would leave busy stuck TRUE and permanently suppress status-line redraws (a self-inflicted denial-of-display, not memory corruption). As shown there is no such non-local exit, so this is dismissed as adequately guarded.

summary: This is Vim's internal win_redr_status() status-line renderer — a UI redraw routine, not a wire-format parser, so it is a low remote-attack surface. It is the 'fixed' variant and is defensively written: every write into the MAXPATHL-sized global NameBuff is size-bounded (guarded space append, capped vim_snprintf calls, get_keymap_str size argument), and a static busy flag correctly guards against statusline-induced recursion. No exploitable memory-safety bug is visible in the shown code. The residual items are latent contracts: the signed 'MAXPATHL - len' size arguments are only safe while len <= MAXPATHL (an invariant get_trans_bufname must uphold), len carries a byte-vs-display-cell dual meaning in the multibyte path, and the buffer/file name shown on the status line is the intended CWE-200 information surface.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Internal redraw entry point; wp is trusted editor state, ignore_pum is a bool flag — the signature itself carries no memory contract.
Working pointer that aliases NameBuff or a string literal; all writes through it are individually bounded.
Signed length/column accumulator whose value drives the MAXPATHL - len size arguments and has a byte-vs-cell dual meaning.
Re-entrancy once-guard; correctly set at line 16 and cleared at line 145 with no leaking early return after it is set.
Establishes the NameBuff/len invariant the rest of the function trusts; source of the CWE-200 name display.
Fixed-size global destination buffer paired with the len length field; all appends and the signed size arithmetic key off MAXPATHL.
Single-byte append is explicitly bounds-checked before writing.
Signed MAXPATHL - len size argument converts to size_t; safe only while len <= MAXPATHL — latent underflow-to-overflow contract.
Advances len by bytes actually written; bounded because the preceding snprintf was capped.
Signed geometry arithmetic, immediately clamped to >= (w_width+1)/2 and checked <= 1; feeds only column arguments.
Assigns a display-cell count into len, giving len a unit that differs from the byte-length branches; fragile but not currently unsafe.
Multibyte scan is bounded by the NUL terminator; does not over-read NameBuff.
i is at most the string length, so the truncation-marker write stays inside NameBuff.
Guarded by 'len > this_ru_col - 1', so the positive shift is smaller than len and stays within the buffer.
Writes to the screen grid bounded by p's NUL terminator; not a buffer write into NameBuff.
Uses len as a column offset; columns are geometry-bounded, correctness handled by screen_fill.
Passes the explicit MAXPATHL size limit; write into NameBuff is bounded by the callee's contract.
Room check before drawing keymap; comparison in signed int of bounded column values.
Delegates ruler drawing; no data from this function crosses a boundary unsafely.
Draws a single fill character at a geometry-derived column; no buffer handling.

findings.title: Signed 'MAXPATHL - len' size passed to vim_snprintf assumes len <= MAXPATHL
findings.bug_class: Integer underflow in size argument -> potential heap/global buffer overflow
findings.invariant: len must be non-negative and <= MAXPATHL at each of the four appends (lines 59, 65, 75, 80) so that MAXPATHL - len is a valid, in-range size_t; get_trans_bufname() must leave NameBuff NUL-terminated and shorter than MAXPATHL.
findings.failure_mode: len is a signed int and the size parameter of vim_snprintf is size_t. If len ever exceeds MAXPATHL, the subtraction MAXPATHL - len is a negative int that converts to a huge size_t, turning the capped snprintf into an unbounded write into the fixed global NameBuff and corrupting adjacent memory. In the shown code len is kept in range by the initial STRLEN < MAXPATHL, the guarded space append (line 55), and each capped snprintf, so the bug is latent and depends on the get_trans_bufname() contract; a regression that lets len reach MAXPATHL would immediately weaponize it.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm get_trans_bufname() cannot leave NameBuff with STRLEN >= MAXPATHL and always NUL-terminates, and that no path can make len reach or exceed MAXPATHL before any of the four vim_snprintf calls.

findings.title: len holds display-cell count in the multibyte branch but byte length elsewhere
findings.bug_class: Unit confusion (display cells vs bytes) in a length variable
findings.invariant: len must be used only as a display-COLUMN quantity after line 104 (as screen_fill and the keymap room check do); no consumer may treat len as a byte index into p.
findings.failure_mode: In the has_mbyte branch len is assigned clen, a display-cell count from mb_string2cells(), whereas at line 47 and line 117 len is a byte length. It is currently used downstream only as a column offset (line 121) and in a column comparison (line 125), so no out-of-bounds access occurs today. The risk is that the dual meaning is fragile: any future or refactored use of len as a byte offset into p on the multibyte path would index the wrong position and could over-read or over-write. Impact today is at most an incorrect status-line fill width.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify that after line 104 no code path uses len as a byte index/length into p or NameBuff, and that screen_fill treats 'len + wp->w_wincol' strictly as a column.

findings.title: Buffer/file name and keymap rendered onto the status line (information surface)
findings.bug_class: Information exposure through UI rendering (by design)
findings.invariant: get_trans_bufname() and get_keymap_str() must never overflow or leave NameBuff unterminated; the displayed content is intentionally the buffer name plus state flags.
findings.failure_mode: The routine copies the buffer's (possibly attacker-influenced, e.g. a hostile filename opened in the editor) name into NameBuff and paints it, along with [Help]/[Preview]/[+]/[RO] flags and a keymap string, onto the visible status line. This is expected editor behavior, not a code defect; it is called out only because the sample is tagged as an information-exposure case. The only way this becomes a memory-safety issue is if get_trans_bufname()/get_keymap_str() fail their size/termination contract, which would turn the STRLEN calls into over-reads (CWE-125).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the name/keymap helpers respect MAXPATHL and NUL-terminate; confirm there is no sensitive data path (e.g. full paths of restricted files) that policy requires masking on the status line.

audit_checklist: Prove len can never reach or exceed MAXPATHL before any of the four vim_snprintf(p+len, MAXPATHL-len, ...) calls (lines 59, 65, 75, 80).
Verify get_trans_bufname() writes a NUL-terminated string of length < MAXPATHL into NameBuff and cannot overflow it.
Verify get_keymap_str(wp, "<%s>", NameBuff, MAXPATHL) honors the MAXPATHL size argument and NUL-terminates, given it reuses the same global NameBuff.
Check that after 'len = clen' (line 104) len is only ever consumed as a display column, never as a byte index into p.
Confirm the multibyte scan loop (lines 101-103) terminates on NUL for every possible byte sequence, including malformed/truncated multibyte input in NameBuff.
Confirm none of redraw_custom_statusline, screen_*, or win_redr_ruler can exit non-locally (longjmp/exception) and leave the static 'busy' flag stuck TRUE.

Confirm the column arguments to screen_fill/screen_puts (len + wp->w_wincol, this_ru_col + wp->w_wincol, this_ru_col - STRLEN(NameBuff) - 1 + wp->w_wincol) stay within the screen grid for all window geometries.
